message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Dysregulated IL-1β and IL-6 responses have been implicated in the pathogenesis of severe Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19). Innovative approaches for evaluating the biological activity of these cytokines
                  <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                  are urgently needed to complement clinical trials of therapeutic targeting of IL-1β and IL-6 in COVID-19. We show that the expression of IL-1β or IL-6 inducible transcriptional signatures (modules) reflects the bioactivity of these cytokines in immunopathology modelled by juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) and rheumatoid arthritis. In COVID-19, elevated expression of IL-1β and IL-6 response modules, but not the cytokine transcripts themselves, is a feature of infection in the nasopharynx and blood, but is not associated with severity of COVID-19 disease, length of stay or mortality. We propose that IL-1β and IL-6 transcriptional response modules provide a dynamic readout of functional cytokine activity
                  <jats:italic>in vivo</jats:italic>
                  , aiding quantification of the biological effects of immunomodulatory therapies in COVID-19.
                </jats:p>
title: Transcriptional response modules characterise IL-1β and IL-6 activity in COVID-19
